title: Meta's Next Headset Reportedly Will Not Be Called Quest 4. The Brand Reset Is Overdue.
author: Alex Reeves
snippet: Leaker Nima Zeighami says Meta's next-gen headset, codename Pismo, will not ship as Quest 4. Andrew Bosworth has confirmed two devices. The branding question matters more than it looks.
body:
Nima Zeighami, one of the more consistently accurate VR hardware leakers, claimed this week that Meta's next-generation standalone headset, internally codenamed Pismo, will not ship under the Quest 4 name. Meta CTO Andrew Bosworth has separately confirmed on Instagram that Meta is working on two distinct devices in the Pismo family, with Pismo High and Pismo Low variants targeting different price points. None of this is a full product announcement, but the combination of an experienced leaker calling the naming shift and Meta's own CTO confirming the two-device structure is a stronger signal than the usual rumor-cycle noise. If Meta is rebranding, it is worth asking why, and what the answer says about where Quest goes from here.

The Quest brand has done its job

The Quest brand launched in 2019 with the original Oculus Quest, which was the first credible standalone VR headset. Quest 2 in 2020 was a genuine crossover hit, selling an estimated 20 million units and establishing VR as a mainstream gaming category for the first time. Quest 3 in 2023 doubled down on mixed reality with full color passthrough. Quest 3S in 2024 hit the entry-level price point. At this point the Quest name is the most recognizable VR brand in the consumer market, bigger than PSVR, bigger than Vision Pro, bigger than every competitor combined.

Which is exactly why rebranding would make sense. The Quest lineup has fragmented into at least four active SKUs: Quest 2 still selling at discount, Quest 3 as the flagship, Quest 3S as the budget option, and Quest Pro as the discontinued enterprise tier. Retail buyers already have trouble telling them apart. A Quest 4 launched into that lineup would have to explain why it is not a Quest 3S Plus or a Quest Pro 2, and the answer would probably not land cleanly in a 30-second ad.

Two devices, two tiers

Bosworth's confirmation of Pismo High and Pismo Low is the structural piece that matters. Meta is not launching one device to replace Quest 3. They are launching a premium option and a mainstream option in the same family, which is how Apple splits iPhone, how Sony splits PlayStation, and how every mature hardware category eventually segments. If Pismo High is the $800 enthusiast headset and Pismo Low is the $400 mainstream device, the current Quest 3 and Quest 3S naming split does not scale to two entirely new products without getting confusing.

A new brand also gives Meta cover to reset expectations. The Quest brand carries consumer associations with gaming, which is both an asset and a limit. If Meta wants to position the higher-end Pismo as a productivity and mixed reality device rather than a gaming headset, a new name makes that pivot easier. Calling it Quest 4 Pro locks it into the gaming narrative. Calling it something new opens the door to telling a different story, closer to how Vision Pro avoided being marketed as a VR headset at all.

What the branding options probably look like

Meta has not telegraphed a direction. The most obvious options are a completely new brand, like the Horizon OS sub-brand Meta has already established, or a more generic Meta-something naming scheme that ties the hardware more tightly to the parent company. "Meta Horizon" is already the name of Meta's VR operating system, and extending it to hardware would be cleanest. "Meta Vision" would be an obvious shot at Apple but runs into trademark issues. "Meta Pismo" is plausible but only if they can sell consumers on the new name, which is a real lift.

There is also the possibility that Meta uses the rebrand to split the two tiers more visibly. Pismo High as "Meta Horizon" and Pismo Low as a retained Quest 4 might let Meta keep the mainstream Quest brand for the affordable headset while using a new name for the premium tier. That approach mirrors what Microsoft did with Surface, where Surface Pro and Surface are clearly different tiers with different target buyers. The risk is that splitting the brand further confuses buyers rather than clarifying.

Why the competitive timing is right

Samsung Galaxy XR ships this year on Android XR. Apple is pivoting Vision Pro away from consumer push and toward enterprise and healthcare. Sony has PSVR2 holding steady but not expanding aggressively. Bytedance's Pico 4 Ultra is growing in Europe and Asia. The competitive landscape is about to look less like Meta-and-everyone-else and more like a genuine multi-platform market.

Meta launching its next-generation hardware under a fresh brand gives it room to position against Samsung and Apple on different grounds than Quest 3 was positioned against Quest 2. The Quest brand was built around the idea of being the standalone VR headset. The next-generation device needs to be positioned against Galaxy XR and whatever Apple does next, and that conversation is about mixed reality, spatial computing, AI integration, and premium software experiences. A new brand helps Meta frame that conversation on its preferred terms.

What we do not know yet

The launch timing, the pricing, the final specs, and the actual brand name are all still speculative. Zeighami has a reasonable track record but his leaks are typically several months ahead of any official confirmation. Bosworth's Instagram comments are signal but not an announcement. Meta Connect in the fall is the most likely venue for a formal reveal, and that is still six months away.

What is not speculative is that the Quest lineup needs a reset. Whether the reset comes as a full rebrand, a split brand, or a surprise Quest 4 announcement that pretends the fragmentation problem does not exist, the next 12 months will tell us which direction Meta chooses. My bet is on the rebrand. The Quest name has done its work. It is probably time to give it a graceful exit.
